prompts.title: Journal Entries for Indian Trading Business
prompts.prompt: Act as a Chartered Accountant and commerce tutor with 15 years experience teaching CA Foundation and B.Com students in India. I need you to prepare complete journal entries for a Mumbai-based trading business for the month of April 2026. Here is the context: Sharma Traders (sole proprietorship) deals in electronics. Transactions include: (1) Started business with INR 10,00,000 cash and INR 5,00,000 bank balance, (2) Purchased goods worth INR 2,36,000 including 18% GST from Delhi supplier on credit, (3) Sold goods for INR 1,77,000 including 18% GST to a Mumbai retailer for cash, (4) Paid rent INR 25,000 by cheque, (5) Purchased furniture INR 50,000 plus 18% GST, (6) Paid salary INR 40,000, (7) Bank charges INR 500, (8) Owner withdrew INR 15,000 for personal use. Please provide: (1) Complete journal entries with narrations in proper Indian format, (2) Separate CGST/SGST/IGST ledger posting logic, (3) Treatment of input tax credit, (4) Common mistakes students make. Format as a properly aligned journal table with Date, Particulars, L.F., Debit (INR), Credit (INR). Follow ICAI study material conventions and Ind AS terminology.

prompts.title: GST Computation and GSTR-3B Preparation
prompts.prompt: Act as a GST practitioner with 10 years experience filing returns for Indian SMEs. I need you to compute GST liability and prepare a GSTR-3B summary for a Bengaluru-based IT services company for March 2026. Here is the context: Infotech Solutions Pvt Ltd has the following data: Outward taxable supplies (B2B) INR 45,00,000, B2C INR 8,00,000, Export of services (zero-rated) INR 12,00,000, Inter-state supplies to Tamil Nadu INR 15,00,000. Inward supplies: Intra-state purchases INR 20,00,000, Inter-state INR 6,00,000, Import of services INR 4,00,000 (reverse charge), Capital goods INR 5,00,000. Applicable rate is 18%. Opening ITC balance: CGST INR 50,000, SGST INR 50,000, IGST INR 1,00,000. Please provide: (1) Tax liability computation (CGST/SGST/IGST split), (2) Eligible ITC with reverse charge treatment, (3) Net GST payable in cash, (4) Table 3.1, 3.2, 4, and 6.1 of GSTR-3B filled with values, (5) Due date and late fee implications. Format as an ICAI-style solved problem with step-by-step workings in INR. Reference CGST Act 2017 sections where relevant.

prompts.title: Rectification of Errors Before Trial Balance
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior commerce faculty with 12 years experience preparing students for CA Foundation Paper 1. I need you to solve a rectification of errors problem. Here is the context: While preparing the trial balance of Patel Enterprises, Ahmedabad as on 31 March 2026, the bookkeeper found these errors: (1) Purchase of goods from Ramesh INR 8,500 recorded as INR 5,800, (2) Sales book undercast by INR 3,200, (3) Wages paid for machinery installation INR 12,000 charged to wages account, (4) Discount allowed INR 450 posted to discount received account, (5) Goods returned by customer INR 2,100 recorded only in customer account, (6) Cash sales INR 15,000 not recorded at all, (7) Depreciation on furniture INR 6,000 omitted. Please provide: (1) Classification of each error (one-sided or two-sided, error of principle/commission/omission), (2) Rectification journal entries with narrations, (3) Impact on profit before and after rectification, (4) Suspense account if applicable. Format as a table followed by journal entries in INR. Reference ICAI study material approach and highlight conceptual traps.

prompts.title: Income Tax Computation Old vs New Regime
prompts.prompt: Act as a tax consultant with 10 years experience advising salaried individuals in India. I need you to compute income tax liability under both old and new regimes for AY 2026-27. Here is the context: Ms. Priya Iyer, age 34, software engineer at TCS Chennai, has: Basic salary INR 14,40,000, HRA INR 5,76,000 (rent paid INR 4,20,000 in Chennai), Special allowance INR 2,40,000, LTA INR 60,000, Employer PF contribution INR 1,72,800. Other income: FD interest INR 85,000, Savings interest INR 12,000, Dividend INR 25,000. Investments/deductions: PPF INR 1,50,000, ELSS INR 50,000, Health insurance self INR 25,000, parents (senior citizen) INR 45,000, Home loan interest INR 2,10,000 (self-occupied), NPS 80CCD(1B) INR 50,000. Please provide: (1) Gross total income computation, (2) Old regime tax with all Chapter VI-A deductions, (3) New regime tax per Section 115BAC, (4) Surcharge and cess calculation, (5) Recommendation with rationale. Format as side-by-side comparison table in INR. Reference Income Tax Act 1961 sections.

prompts.title: Vouching Audit Procedure for Cash Book
prompts.prompt: Act as an audit manager at a mid-sized CA firm in Pune with 12 years experience conducting statutory audits under Companies Act 2013. I need you to prepare a detailed vouching program for the cash book of Kirloskar Components Pvt Ltd for FY 2025-26. Here is the context: The company has annual turnover of INR 85 crore, maintains books in Tally Prime, and has three bank accounts (HDFC, SBI, ICICI) plus petty cash. Previous year audit flagged weak internal controls on cash payments above INR 10,000. Please provide: (1) Vouching objectives for cash receipts and cash payments separately, (2) Sample documents to verify for each transaction type (receipts, payments, contra, journal), (3) Specific checks for Section 40A(3) compliance (cash payment limit INR 10,000), (4) Red flags and fraud indicators to watch, (5) Audit working paper template with columns, (6) SA 500 audit evidence considerations, (7) Questions for management. Format as a structured audit program document suitable for CA Inter/Final students. Reference SA standards issued by ICAI and relevant Companies Act sections.

prompts.title: Marginal Costing Break-Even Analysis
prompts.prompt: Act as a cost accounting professor with 15 years experience teaching CMA and CA Inter students. I need you to solve a comprehensive marginal costing problem. Here is the context: Godrej Appliances manufactures microwave ovens in its Mohali plant. Data for 2025-26: Selling price INR 8,500 per unit, Direct material INR 3,200, Direct labour INR 1,100, Variable overhead INR 700, Fixed overheads INR 2,40,00,000 per annum, Current production 12,000 units. Management is considering: (a) a 10% price cut to boost volume by 25%, (b) expanding capacity by INR 60,00,000 fixed cost addition to produce 18,000 units at current price, (c) accepting a special export order of 2,000 units at INR 6,000 per unit with additional packaging cost INR 200 per unit. Please provide: (1) Current contribution per unit, P/V ratio, BEP in units and INR, margin of safety, (2) Evaluation of each alternative with incremental analysis, (3) Recommendation with reasoning, (4) Sensitivity table at different volumes. Format as ICAI-style solved problem with workings in INR. Highlight CVP assumptions.

prompts.title: Ratio Analysis of Reliance Industries
prompts.prompt: Act as an equity research analyst and CFA with 10 years experience analyzing Indian listed companies. I need you to perform comprehensive ratio analysis of Reliance Industries Limited using FY 2024-25 annual report figures. Here is the context: Use these assumed figures (INR crore): Revenue 9,00,000, EBITDA 1,65,000, PAT 75,000, Total Assets 16,50,000, Total Equity 8,50,000, Total Debt 3,20,000, Current Assets 2,40,000, Current Liabilities 2,10,000, Inventory 1,20,000, Trade Receivables 80,000, Interest 22,000, Share Capital (face value INR 10) 6,766 crore shares, Market price INR 2,950. Please provide: (1) Liquidity ratios (current, quick, cash), (2) Solvency ratios (debt-equity, interest coverage, DSCR), (3) Profitability ratios (GP, NP, ROE, ROCE, ROA), (4) Efficiency ratios (inventory turnover, debtors days, asset turnover), (5) Valuation ratios (EPS, P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA), (6) Comparison with industry benchmarks for oil and gas/telecom conglomerates, (7) Interpretation and red flags. Format as a structured table with formulas, calculations, and commentary. Reference Ind AS disclosures.

prompts.title: Depreciation Computation Under Companies Act 2013
prompts.prompt: Act as a CA with 12 years of corporate audit experience at a Big 4 firm in Gurgaon. I need you to compute depreciation under Schedule II of Companies Act 2013 using both SLM and WDV methods. Here is the context: Bharat Manufacturing Ltd acquired the following fixed assets during FY 2025-26: (1) Factory building INR 2,50,00,000 on 1 April 2025 (useful life 30 years), (2) Plant and machinery INR 80,00,000 on 1 July 2025 (useful life 15 years), (3) Office computers INR 12,00,000 on 15 September 2025 (useful life 3 years), (4) Motor vehicles INR 35,00,000 on 1 January 2026 (useful life 8 years), (5) Furniture INR 8,00,000 on 1 April 2025 (useful life 10 years). Residual value is 5% for all assets. Please provide: (1) Depreciation for FY 2025-26 under SLM and WDV separately with pro-rata calculations, (2) Journal entries for depreciation, (3) Fixed asset schedule as per Schedule III disclosure, (4) Treatment if useful life differs from Schedule II, (5) Impact on deferred tax (Section 32 of Income Tax Act vs Companies Act). Format as a tabular schedule in INR with workings.

prompts.title: Bank Reconciliation Statement Preparation
prompts.prompt: Act as an accounts teacher with 10 years experience preparing Class 12 CBSE and B.Com first year students. I need you to prepare a bank reconciliation statement from given data. Here is the context: Mehta Stationers, Jaipur shows cash book (bank column) balance of INR 1,25,680 debit on 31 March 2026, while the passbook shows INR 1,48,320 credit. On comparison: (1) Cheques issued INR 42,500 but not yet presented, (2) Cheques deposited INR 28,400 not yet cleared, (3) Bank charges INR 320 not recorded in cash book, (4) Interest credited by bank INR 1,850 not in cash book, (5) Dividend directly collected by bank INR 4,200, (6) Standing instruction for insurance premium INR 6,800 paid by bank, (7) A customer directly deposited INR 12,000 not yet intimated, (8) Cheque of INR 5,500 dishonoured, recorded in passbook only, (9) Wrong debit by bank INR 2,000. Please provide: (1) BRS starting from cash book balance, (2) BRS starting from passbook balance, (3) Adjusted cash book if applicable, (4) Common student errors, (5) Conceptual explanation of each adjustment. Format as two-column BRS in INR.

prompts.title: Partnership Admission Revaluation and Goodwill
prompts.prompt: Act as a commerce professor with 18 years experience teaching partnership accounts. I need you to solve an admission of partner problem. Here is the context: Arjun and Bhavna are partners in a Kolkata textile firm sharing profits 3:2. Their balance sheet as on 31 March 2026 shows: Capital Arjun INR 6,00,000, Bhavna INR 4,00,000, Creditors INR 1,50,000, Reserve INR 1,00,000, Building INR 5,00,000, Machinery INR 3,50,000, Debtors INR 2,00,000 (provision INR 10,000), Stock INR 1,40,000, Cash INR 70,000. Chitra is admitted for 1/4 share on 1 April 2026 on these terms: (1) Chitra brings INR 3,00,000 capital and INR 80,000 goodwill in cash, (2) Building revalued at INR 6,00,000, machinery depreciated by 10%, (3) Provision for doubtful debts raised to 8% of debtors, (4) Creditors include INR 15,000 not payable, (5) Outstanding repairs INR 8,000. Please provide: (1) Revaluation account, (2) Partners capital accounts, (3) New balance sheet, (4) New profit-sharing ratio and sacrifice ratio, (5) Treatment of goodwill per AS 26. Format as proper partnership accounting statements in INR.

prompts.title: Cash Flow Statement as per Ind AS 7
prompts.prompt: Act as a financial reporting specialist with 12 years of Ind AS implementation experience at Indian listed companies. I need you to prepare a cash flow statement for TechNova Solutions Ltd for FY 2025-26 using the indirect method as per Ind AS 7. Here is the context: Comparative data (INR lakhs): PAT 480, Depreciation 120, Interest expense 55, Interest income 18, Loss on sale of machinery 12, Tax expense 160. Balance sheet changes: Inventory increased 85, Trade receivables increased 140, Trade payables increased 60, Prepaid expenses decreased 15, Provisions increased 25. Investing: Machinery sold (book value 50) for 38, New plant purchased 350, Investment in equity 100. Financing: Fresh equity issued 200, Long-term borrowing repaid 180, Dividend paid 90, Interest paid 55. Opening cash and equivalents 115. Please provide: (1) Operating activities with PAT reconciliation, (2) Investing activities, (3) Financing activities, (4) Net change and closing cash, (5) Notes on Ind AS 7 classification choices (interest, dividend), (6) Differences from AS 3. Format as a proper cash flow statement in INR lakhs with workings.

prompts.title: TDS Computation and Return Filing Scenarios
prompts.prompt: Act as a tax compliance officer with 9 years experience handling TDS for Indian corporates. I need you to compute TDS liability and classify returns for Q4 FY 2025-26. Here is the context: Surya Manufacturing Pvt Ltd, Coimbatore made these payments in Q4: (1) Salary to 45 employees totalling INR 82,00,000 (Section 192), (2) Contractor payment to Ramesh Constructions INR 6,50,000 (Section 194C), (3) Professional fees to CA firm INR 2,80,000 (Section 194J), (4) Rent for office INR 4,20,000 to individual landlord (Section 194I), (5) Commission to agent INR 95,000 (Section 194H), (6) Interest on FD to NBFC INR 1,85,000 (Section 194A), (7) Payment to non-resident for software INR 15,00,000 (Section 195), (8) Purchase of goods from vendor INR 75,00,000 (Section 194Q). Please provide: (1) TDS rate and amount for each payment, (2) Threshold applicability, (3) Applicable form (24Q/26Q/27Q), (4) Due dates for deposit and return filing, (5) Consequences of non-compliance (interest, penalty, disallowance under 40(a)(ia)), (6) TDS certificate format. Format as a comprehensive table in INR.

prompts.title: Standard Costing Variance Analysis
prompts.prompt: Act as a cost and management accountant with 14 years of manufacturing industry experience. I need you to compute and interpret standard costing variances for Ashok Leyland's truck chassis production unit at Hosur. Here is the context: Standard data per chassis: Direct material 800 kg at INR 65 per kg, Direct labour 40 hours at INR 250 per hour, Variable overhead INR 180 per labour hour, Fixed overhead budgeted INR 48,00,000 per month for 400 chassis. Actual data for March 2026: Production 380 chassis, Material used 3,10,000 kg at INR 68 per kg, Labour hours 15,800 at INR 245 per hour, Variable overhead INR 29,50,000, Fixed overhead INR 49,20,000. Please provide: (1) Material cost variance, price variance, usage variance, (2) Labour cost variance, rate variance, efficiency variance, idle time (if any), (3) Variable overhead variances (expenditure and efficiency), (4) Fixed overhead variances (expenditure, volume, capacity, efficiency), (5) Reconciliation from standard cost to actual cost, (6) Interpretation and action points for plant manager. Format as an ICAI-style solved problem in INR with workings and a reconciliation statement.

prompts.title: Common-Size Financial Statement Analysis
prompts.prompt: Act as a financial analyst with 8 years experience preparing credit appraisal reports at an Indian public sector bank. I need you to prepare common-size financial statements and analyze HUL vs ITC for FY 2024-25. Here is the context: HUL figures (INR crore): Revenue 62,000, COGS 31,000, Employee cost 2,800, Other expenses 15,200, Depreciation 1,100, Interest 180, Tax 3,150, PAT 8,570. Total assets 52,000, Equity 48,500, Debt 800, Current liabilities 12,500. ITC figures: Revenue 72,000, COGS 28,000, Excise and GST 18,500, Employee cost 3,200, Other 9,800, Depreciation 1,850, Interest 50, Tax 5,400, PAT 20,200. Total assets 92,000, Equity 78,000, Debt 350. Please provide: (1) Common-size P&L (each item as % of revenue) for both companies side by side, (2) Common-size balance sheet, (3) Trend and comparison commentary, (4) Business model insight (FMCG vs diversified), (5) Strengths and weaknesses in cost structure, (6) Credit rating perspective. Format as two comparative tables with analytical narrative in INR crore.

prompts.title: Process Costing with Equivalent Units
prompts.prompt: Act as a CMA faculty with 13 years experience teaching process costing to CA Inter and CMA Inter students. I need you to solve a process costing problem with opening WIP using FIFO method. Here is the context: Rasayan Chemicals Ltd, Vadodara has a three-process manufacturing setup. For Process II in March 2026: Opening WIP 2,000 units (60% complete for material, 40% for labour and overhead) valued at material INR 48,000, labour INR 15,000, overhead INR 12,000, previous process cost INR 80,000. Units received from Process I 18,000 at INR 9,00,000. Units transferred to Process III 17,500. Closing WIP 2,000 units (80% material, 50% labour and overhead). Normal loss 5% of input realising INR 20 per unit scrap. Current period costs: Material INR 4,68,000, Labour INR 2,25,000, Overhead INR 1,80,000. Please provide: (1) Statement of equivalent production under FIFO, (2) Cost per equivalent unit for each cost element, (3) Valuation of output transferred and closing WIP, (4) Process II account, (5) Abnormal loss/gain treatment. Format as ICAI-style solved problem in INR. Explain FIFO vs weighted average differences.

prompts.title: Ind AS 115 Revenue Recognition Five-Step Model
prompts.prompt: Act as a financial reporting specialist with Big 4 experience implementing Ind AS 115 at Indian software and real estate companies. I need you to apply the five-step revenue recognition model to a complex contract. Here is the context: Infosys Ltd enters into a contract with a UK client on 1 April 2025 for INR 24 crore spanning 18 months. Deliverables: (1) Software licence (distinct) standalone price INR 8 crore, (2) Implementation services INR 10 crore (6 months), (3) Two years of post-contract support INR 6 crore, (4) Customization work INR 4 crore (not distinct from licence). Client paid INR 6 crore upfront, INR 12 crore after implementation, INR 6 crore over support period. Discount of INR 4 crore allocated across performance obligations. Please provide: (1) Step 1 - contract identification and combinations, (2) Step 2 - performance obligations identification with distinct criteria, (3) Step 3 - transaction price including variable consideration, (4) Step 4 - allocation using relative standalone selling price, (5) Step 5 - recognition pattern (point in time vs over time), (6) Journal entries and disclosures, (7) Contract asset vs receivable. Format as a technical memo in INR crore referencing paragraphs of Ind AS 115.

prompts.title: Verification of Fixed Assets Audit
prompts.prompt: Act as an audit senior at an ICAI-empanelled CA firm in Hyderabad with 8 years experience auditing manufacturing companies. I need you to prepare a complete verification procedure for fixed assets of a medium-sized company. Here is the context: You are auditing Pragati Engineering Ltd, Visakhapatnam for FY 2025-26. Gross block is INR 185 crore with major additions of INR 22 crore (CNC machines) and deletions of INR 5 crore during the year. The company follows component accounting per Schedule II and capitalizes borrowing costs. Please provide: (1) Audit objectives (existence, ownership, valuation, completeness, disclosure), (2) Verification procedures for opening balances, additions, disposals, and impairment, (3) Physical verification plan with sampling, (4) Title deed verification for land and buildings, (5) Compliance check with Ind AS 16 and Schedule II, (6) CARO 2020 reporting requirements (clause 3(i)), (7) Ind AS 36 impairment testing procedures, (8) Working paper template, (9) Common observations for management letter. Format as a structured audit program for CA Inter/Final students referencing SA 500, SA 530, SA 501.

prompts.title: GSTR-1 and GSTR-9 Annual Return Reconciliation
prompts.prompt: Act as a GST practitioner with 8 years experience preparing annual returns for Indian SMEs and corporates. I need you to reconcile GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, and books of account for preparing GSTR-9 for FY 2024-25. Here is the context: Sundaram Fasteners Ltd, Chennai has these figures (INR): Outward supplies as per books 42,50,00,000, GSTR-1 total 42,35,00,000, GSTR-3B total 42,65,00,000. ITC as per books 4,80,00,000, GSTR-3B claimed 4,92,00,000, GSTR-2B available 4,75,00,000. Credit notes issued INR 65,00,000 (15,00,000 after September following FY). Export with LUT INR 8,50,00,000. Advances received but not adjusted INR 28,00,000. Please provide: (1) Reconciliation of turnover (books vs GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B), (2) ITC reconciliation with GSTR-2B, (3) Table 9, 14, 17, and 18 of GSTR-9 with figures, (4) Adjustments for prior period, (5) Common errors students/practitioners make, (6) Whether GSTR-9C is applicable and reconciliation items, (7) Late fee and interest implications. Format as structured reconciliation tables in INR. Reference relevant CGST Rules.

prompts.title: Trading and Profit Loss Account Preparation
prompts.prompt: Act as a B.Com financial accounting professor with 11 years experience at Delhi University affiliated colleges. I need you to prepare Trading and Profit and Loss Account and Balance Sheet from a trial balance with adjustments. Here is the context: Trial balance of Kumar Enterprises, Lucknow as on 31 March 2026 (INR): Capital 8,00,000 (Cr), Drawings 60,000, Opening stock 1,20,000, Purchases 14,50,000, Sales 22,80,000, Purchase returns 25,000, Sales returns 35,000, Wages 85,000, Salaries 1,40,000, Rent 72,000, Insurance 18,000, Carriage inward 12,000, Carriage outward 22,000, Advertisement 45,000, Debtors 2,80,000, Creditors 1,95,000, Bad debts 8,000, Cash 55,000, Bank 1,40,000, Furniture 1,20,000, Machinery 3,50,000, Bills receivable 40,000, Bills payable 32,000. Adjustments: (1) Closing stock INR 1,85,000, (2) Outstanding salaries INR 15,000, (3) Prepaid insurance INR 4,000, (4) Depreciate furniture 10%, machinery 15%, (5) Provision for doubtful debts 5% on debtors, (6) Goods worth INR 10,000 taken by owner. Please provide Trading A/c, P&L A/c, Balance Sheet with adjustment entries explained. Format in INR per Indian GAAP.

prompts.title: Capital Gains Tax on Property Sale
prompts.prompt: Act as a tax consultant with 12 years experience advising HNIs on capital gains in India. I need you to compute capital gains tax for AY 2026-27. Here is the context: Mr. Rajesh Gupta, resident in Mumbai, sold the following during FY 2025-26: (1) Residential flat in Andheri purchased 15 June 2010 for INR 42,00,000 (stamp duty and registration INR 3,00,000), sold on 20 November 2025 for INR 1,85,00,000 (brokerage INR 1,85,000), (2) Listed equity shares of Infosys purchased 5 August 2019 for INR 6,50,000, sold 10 February 2026 for INR 14,20,000 (STT paid), (3) Debt mutual funds purchased 1 April 2022 for INR 8,00,000, redeemed 15 March 2026 for INR 10,40,000, (4) Gold jewellery (inherited from father in 1995, FMV on 1 April 2001 INR 2,50,000) sold for INR 12,00,000 on 25 December 2025. CII: 2001-02=100, 2010-11=167, 2024-25=363, 2025-26=376. Please provide: (1) Classification as LTCG/STCG for each, (2) Indexed cost computation, (3) Tax rate under Section 112/112A/111A/50AA, (4) Exemption options (54, 54F, 54EC) with conditions, (5) Final tax liability in INR. Reference Income Tax Act sections.

prompts.title: Adjusting Entries at Year End
prompts.prompt: Act as a senior accountant at a Chennai-based mid-size company with 10 years experience in month-end and year-end closing. I need you to prepare year-end adjusting journal entries. Here is the context: Krishna Exports Pvt Ltd is closing books for 31 March 2026. Unadjusted data reveals: (1) Salaries of INR 1,80,000 for March unpaid, (2) Rent received in advance INR 60,000 pertaining to April-May 2026, (3) Insurance premium INR 48,000 paid on 1 October 2025 for one year, (4) Interest accrued on FD INR 32,000 not received, (5) Commission earned but not billed INR 25,000, (6) Bad debts to be written off INR 18,000, new provision at 5% on debtors of INR 8,00,000, (7) Depreciation on machinery at 15% WDV on opening balance INR 24,00,000 (additions INR 6,00,000 on 1 October 2025), (8) Goods worth INR 35,000 sent on approval not yet approved, (9) GST payable INR 1,20,000 to be recognised, (10) Provision for tax INR 4,50,000. Please provide: (1) All adjusting journal entries with narrations, (2) Concept (accrual, matching, prudence) behind each, (3) Impact on P&L and Balance Sheet, (4) Common errors. Format as journal table in INR.

prompts.title: E-Way Bill and Invoice Generation Scenarios
prompts.prompt: Act as a GST compliance expert with 7 years experience implementing e-invoicing and e-way bill systems at Indian businesses. I need you to analyze e-way bill and e-invoice requirements across scenarios. Here is the context: Maharashtra Logistics Ltd handles dispatches for various clients. Analyze these five scenarios for March 2026: (1) Intra-state movement Mumbai to Pune, invoice value INR 45,000, (2) Inter-state Mumbai to Ahmedabad, invoice value INR 72,000, (3) Stock transfer between branches within Maharashtra, value INR 2,50,000, (4) Job work material sent to Nashik worth INR 1,80,000, (5) Client with turnover INR 8 crore issuing B2B invoice of INR 1,20,000, (6) Client with turnover INR 12 crore issuing B2B invoice of INR 85,000. Please provide: (1) E-way bill applicability, threshold, and validity period for each, (2) E-invoice mandatory or not (IRN generation), (3) Documents to accompany movement, (4) Part A and Part B responsibilities, (5) Consequences of non-compliance and penalty under Section 129, (6) Recent notifications on threshold changes, (7) Practical tips for transporters. Format as a comparative table in INR with references to CGST Rules 138 and 48.

prompts.title: Inventory Valuation FIFO Weighted Average
prompts.prompt: Act as a cost accountant with 10 years experience in inventory management and FMCG costing. I need you to compute inventory valuation using FIFO and weighted average methods as per Ind AS 2. Here is the context: Britannia Industries Ltd, Bengaluru plant has these stock movements for Maida (raw material) in March 2026: Opening stock 5,000 kg at INR 45 per kg. Purchases: 4 March 8,000 kg at INR 47, 11 March 6,000 kg at INR 48, 18 March 10,000 kg at INR 46, 25 March 4,000 kg at INR 49. Issues to production: 6 March 7,000 kg, 13 March 9,000 kg, 20 March 8,000 kg, 28 March 5,000 kg. Please provide: (1) Stock ledger under FIFO method showing running balance, (2) Stock ledger under weighted average method, (3) Closing stock valuation under both methods, (4) Cost of materials consumed under both, (5) Impact on profit and tax, (6) Which method is permitted under Ind AS 2 and Income Tax Act, (7) Practical considerations in Tally Prime implementation, (8) LIFO prohibition rationale. Format as a stock ledger in INR with commentary.

prompts.title: Audit Report Drafting Modified Opinion
prompts.prompt: Act as an audit partner at a mid-sized CA firm with 18 years experience signing statutory audit reports under Companies Act 2013. I need you to draft an audit report for a private limited company with specific issues. Here is the context: You are auditing Shakti Textiles Pvt Ltd, Surat for FY 2025-26. Key findings: (1) Inventory of INR 8 crore not physically verified at some locations due to access restrictions, (2) Management refuses to provide confirmations for trade receivables of INR 3.5 crore from a related party, (3) A legal case with potential liability of INR 2 crore not disclosed as contingent liability, (4) Depreciation computed incorrectly causing overstatement of profit by INR 45 lakh (not material to overall financials), (5) Internal financial controls have design weakness in purchase approvals. Please provide: (1) Analysis of each issue - scope limitation vs misstatement, (2) Materiality assessment, (3) Type of modified opinion (qualified/adverse/disclaimer), (4) Complete draft audit report per SA 700/705/706 with Basis for Opinion, Key Audit Matters, Emphasis of Matter, (5) CARO 2020 reporting impact, (6) Section 143(3) reporting on internal controls. Format as a formal audit report document.

prompts.title: Budgetary Control Flexible Budget
prompts.prompt: Act as a management accountant with 11 years experience preparing budgets at Indian manufacturing companies. I need you to prepare a flexible budget and performance report. Here is the context: Tata Motors CV division's Lucknow plant has these budget details for March 2026 at 80% capacity producing 8,000 engine blocks. Fixed costs (per month): Factory rent INR 12,00,000, Supervisory salaries INR 18,00,000, Depreciation INR 25,00,000, Insurance INR 3,00,000. Variable costs per unit: Direct material INR 4,200, Direct labour INR 850, Power INR 340, Consumables INR 180, Variable overhead INR 220. Semi-variable: Maintenance (INR 6,00,000 fixed + INR 75 per unit), Indirect labour (INR 4,00,000 fixed + INR 60 per unit). Actual output in March was 9,200 units with actual costs: materials INR 3,94,00,000, labour INR 82,00,000, variable overheads INR 72,00,000, fixed INR 62,50,000, maintenance INR 13,80,000, indirect labour INR 9,80,000. Please provide: (1) Flexible budget at 70%, 80%, 90%, 100% capacity, (2) Flexed budget at 9,200 units, (3) Performance report comparing flexed budget with actuals, (4) Variance analysis and interpretation, (5) Cost behavior classification. Format as tables in INR.

prompts.title: Internal Audit of Purchase Cycle
prompts.prompt: Act as an internal auditor with 10 years experience at a listed Indian company subject to Section 138 internal audit requirements. I need you to design an internal audit program for the procure-to-pay cycle. Here is the context: Ultratech Cement's Gujarat unit procures raw materials (limestone, gypsum, coal) worth INR 650 crore annually from 180 vendors. The company uses SAP ERP. Recent concerns: vendor master hygiene, duplicate payments, PO splitting to bypass approval limits (INR 10 lakh for manager, INR 50 lakh for GM, above INR 50 lakh needs director approval), quality rejections not adjusted in payments. Please provide: (1) Risk assessment of the purchase cycle with top 10 risks, (2) Control testing procedures for PR to payment, (3) Data analytics tests (Benford analysis, duplicate detection, split PO detection, weekend entries), (4) Vendor master audit checklist, (5) Three-way matching verification, (6) Sample selection methodology, (7) Reporting format with observations, risk rating, recommendations, (8) Linkage to IFC over financial reporting. Format as a structured internal audit program referencing Standards on Internal Audit (SIA) issued by ICAI.

prompts.title: Bill of Exchange Accounting Treatment
prompts.prompt: Act as a commerce teacher with 12 years experience preparing Class 11 and 12 students for CBSE board examinations. I need you to solve a bills of exchange problem covering all key scenarios. Here is the context: On 1 January 2026, Ashwin of Nagpur sold goods worth INR 80,000 to Bhaskar of Raipur and drew a bill for 3 months which Bhaskar accepted. On 4 January 2026, Ashwin: (a) discounted bill with bank at 12% pa, (b) alternatively endorsed to creditor Chetan for INR 79,500. On due date 4 April 2026, scenario analysis: (1) Bill honoured on due date, (2) Bill dishonoured with noting charges INR 500, (3) Bhaskar unable to pay and requests renewal for 2 months with interest at 15%, Bhaskar pays INR 30,000 cash and accepts new bill, (4) Bhaskar becomes insolvent and only 40 paise per rupee recovered. Please provide: (1) Journal entries in books of Ashwin and Bhaskar for each scenario, (2) Ledger accounts where relevant, (3) Calculation of discounting charges and interest, (4) Conceptual explanation of retiring under rebate, (5) Differences between bills payable and notes payable. Format as journal entries in INR with narrations.

prompts.title: Section 44AB Tax Audit Applicability
prompts.prompt: Act as a practising Chartered Accountant with 14 years experience conducting tax audits under Section 44AB. I need you to analyze tax audit applicability and prepare the relevant clauses of Form 3CD. Here is the context: Analyze these five taxpayer scenarios for AY 2026-27: (1) Mr. Singh runs a garment retail business with turnover INR 1.8 crore, cash receipts INR 12 lakh, cash payments INR 8 lakh, (2) Ms. Patel is a freelance architect with professional receipts INR 65 lakh, (3) Bharat Traders LLP has turnover INR 9.5 crore with all digital transactions, (4) Dr. Verma runs a clinic with professional receipts INR 48 lakh and declares income under Section 44ADA at 50%, (5) Nikhil declares presumptive income under 44AD at 8% on turnover of INR 1.5 crore but actual profit is 5%. Please provide: (1) Section 44AB applicability for each (with new threshold analysis including 5% cash transaction condition), (2) Relevant sub-clause triggering audit, (3) Key Form 3CD clauses applicable including Clause 21 (inadmissible expenses), Clause 34 (TDS), Clause 40 (ratios), (4) Form 3CA/3CB applicability, (5) Due dates and penalty under Section 271B. Format as a detailed analysis in INR.

prompts.title: Consolidated Financial Statements Basics
prompts.prompt: Act as a financial reporting expert with 10 years experience preparing consolidated financial statements under Ind AS 110 for Indian listed companies. I need you to prepare a simple consolidation for a holding-subsidiary structure. Here is the context: Wipro Ltd acquired 80% of Shardha Tech Pvt Ltd on 1 April 2025 for INR 240 crore. On acquisition date, Shardha's net assets book value was INR 260 crore (equity capital 100, reserves 160). Fair value adjustments: building undervalued by INR 30 crore, contingent liability INR 10 crore. As on 31 March 2026 standalone figures (INR crore): Wipro revenue 85,000, PAT 12,500, Total assets 1,10,000, Equity 82,000. Shardha revenue 680, PAT 85, Total assets 340, Equity 295. Intercompany: Wipro sold goods worth INR 40 crore to Shardha at 25% margin, INR 12 crore still in closing stock. Dividend INR 20 crore paid by Shardha. Please provide: (1) Goodwill on consolidation computation, (2) Non-controlling interest calculation (at fair value), (3) Consolidation adjustments (intercompany elimination, unrealised profit, dividend), (4) Consolidated P&L and Balance Sheet extracts, (5) Ind AS 110 key concepts applied. Format as a structured workings in INR crore.

prompts.title: House Property Income Computation
prompts.prompt: Act as a tax practitioner with 9 years experience advising individual clients on house property income. I need you to compute income from house property for AY 2026-27 for multiple properties. Here is the context: Mr. Anand Joshi, a Pune-based professional, owns four properties in FY 2025-26: (1) Self-occupied flat in Pune, municipal value INR 3,60,000, fair rent INR 4,20,000, home loan interest paid INR 2,45,000 (loan taken 2018 for purchase), (2) Let-out property in Bengaluru, actual rent INR 40,000 per month, municipal taxes paid INR 45,000, vacant for 2 months, loan interest INR 1,80,000, (3) Deemed let-out property in Nashik, municipal value INR 2,40,000, fair rent INR 2,80,000, standard rent INR 2,60,000, municipal taxes INR 30,000, loan interest INR 90,000, (4) Property used for own business in Mumbai (not to be considered under HP). Please provide: (1) GAV computation for each property, (2) NAV after municipal taxes, (3) Standard deduction at 30%, (4) Interest deduction under Section 24(b) with INR 2,00,000 cap for self-occupied, (5) Total income from house property, (6) Set-off against other heads and carry forward of loss, (7) Old vs new regime impact. Format as a comparative table in INR.

prompts.title: Company Accounts Issue of Shares
prompts.prompt: Act as a commerce professor with 15 years experience teaching company accounts to B.Com and CA Foundation students. I need you to solve an issue of shares problem with all stages and special scenarios. Here is the context: Surya Industries Ltd, Chennai issued 5,00,000 equity shares of INR 10 each at a premium of INR 4 per share payable as: Application INR 4 (including INR 1 premium), Allotment INR 5 (including INR 2 premium), First call INR 3 (including INR 1 premium), Final call INR 2. Applications received for 7,50,000 shares. Allotment made as: full allotment to 1,00,000 shares, nil allotment to 50,000 shares (money refunded), pro-rata to remaining. Excess application money adjusted on allotment. Mr. Ram holding 10,000 shares (applied for 15,000) failed to pay allotment and first call. His shares were forfeited after first call. 7,000 of these forfeited shares were re-issued at INR 8 per share as fully paid. Final call was made and received on remaining shares. Please provide: (1) Journal entries for all stages, (2) Calculation of money adjusted on allotment for pro-rata applicants, (3) Forfeiture account with gain transfer to capital reserve, (4) Balance sheet extract, (5) Compliance with Companies Act 2013 Section 52 (securities premium). Format in INR.

prompts.title: Ind AS 116 Lease Accounting Journal Entries
prompts.prompt: Act as an Ind AS expert with 8 years experience implementing Ind AS 116 at Indian listed companies. I need you to prepare lease accounting entries from lessee perspective. Here is the context: HDFC Bank entered a 6-year lease for office premises in BKC Mumbai on 1 April 2025. Terms: Annual lease rental INR 1,20,00,000 payable at year end, escalation 5% every year, initial direct costs INR 15,00,000, refundable security deposit INR 2,00,00,000, incremental borrowing rate 9% pa. No purchase option, no residual value guarantee. Bank's FY ends 31 March. Please provide: (1) Present value of lease liability computation with year-wise discounting table, (2) Right-of-use asset initial recognition with all components, (3) Depreciation schedule (straight-line over lease term), (4) Interest expense schedule using effective interest method, (5) Journal entries for Year 1 and Year 2 (initial recognition, payment, interest, depreciation), (6) Balance sheet presentation, (7) P&L impact vs old AS 19 operating lease (straight-line rent), (8) Disclosure requirements. Format as tables and journal entries in INR. Reference Ind AS 116 paragraphs.

prompts.title: Working Capital Management Analysis
prompts.prompt: Act as a financial management professor with 13 years experience teaching CA/CMA/MBA students in India. I need you to estimate working capital requirement and analyze the operating cycle for a manufacturing company. Here is the context: Dabur India's Ghaziabad unit plans to produce 2,40,000 units of a herbal product in 2026-27. Per unit cost: Raw material INR 180, Labour INR 80, Overheads INR 60 (including depreciation INR 15). Selling price INR 400. Storage: Raw material 1.5 months, WIP 0.5 month (material 100%, labour and overhead 50%), Finished goods 1 month. Credit period: from suppliers 1 month, to customers 2 months. Cash and bank minimum INR 15 lakh. Production and sales spread evenly. Please provide: (1) Working capital requirement using total approach (cash cost basis), (2) Operating cycle in days, (3) Working capital turnover ratio, (4) Core and variable working capital, (5) Effect of 10% sales increase, (6) Sources of working capital finance in India (CC, OD, bill discounting, factoring), (7) Impact of MPBF (Tandon Committee second method), (8) Recommendations. Format as a statement of working capital with workings in INR.

prompts.title: GST Input Tax Credit Reversal Rules
prompts.prompt: Act as a GST consultant with 8 years experience handling ITC reversal computations for Indian manufacturing and service companies. I need you to compute ITC reversal under Rule 42 and Rule 43 of CGST Rules. Here is the context: Excel Industries Ltd, Aurangabad has mixed supplies in March 2026. Data (INR): Total ITC on inputs and input services INR 18,00,000 of which: exclusively for taxable supplies INR 6,00,000, exclusively for exempt supplies INR 2,00,000, common credit INR 10,00,000 (out of which INR 1,50,000 relates to personal use of director). Total turnover INR 4,80,00,000 of which exempt (interest income, agricultural produce trading) INR 72,00,000. Capital goods ITC of INR 9,00,000 on plant commissioned 1 April 2023 now used 30% for exempt supplies (five-year rule). Please provide: (1) Rule 42 step-by-step reversal computation (T, T1, T2, T3, C1, C2, C3, D1, D2), (2) Rule 43 capital goods reversal with Tm, Tr, Te formulae, (3) Journal entries for ITC reversal, (4) Annual reconciliation requirements, (5) Impact on GSTR-3B Table 4B, (6) Interest implications under Section 50. Format as detailed computation in INR referencing CGST Rules.

prompts.title: Financial Statement Analysis DuPont
prompts.prompt: Act as an equity research analyst with 9 years experience at an Indian brokerage firm covering the IT sector. I need you to conduct DuPont analysis and decompose ROE for two IT companies. Here is the context: Compare TCS and Infosys for FY 2024-25. TCS figures (INR crore): Revenue 2,42,000, PAT 48,800, Total assets 1,52,000, Equity 96,500, Interest 180. Infosys figures: Revenue 1,62,000, PAT 26,800, Total assets 1,28,000, Equity 84,000, Interest 220. Industry average ROE 28%, ROA 18%. Please provide: (1) Three-step DuPont decomposition (Net profit margin x Asset turnover x Equity multiplier) for both, (2) Five-step DuPont extension (tax burden, interest burden, operating margin, asset turnover, leverage), (3) Side-by-side comparison with variance explanation, (4) Which company has superior operational efficiency vs financial leverage, (5) Trend analysis considerations, (6) Ratio limitations (non-financial factors, accounting policy differences), (7) Investment recommendation with reasoning for a retail investor, (8) Ind AS impact on comparability. Format as a structured analyst report with tables in INR crore.

prompts.title: Contract Costing Construction Company
prompts.prompt: Act as a cost accountant with 12 years experience at an Indian infrastructure company such as L&T or Shapoorji Pallonji. I need you to solve a contract costing problem with profit recognition. Here is the context: Bharti Infrastructure Ltd started Contract No. 207 for construction of a flyover in Delhi on 1 April 2025. Contract price INR 45 crore. Expenditure for year ended 31 March 2026 (INR lakh): Materials issued 620, Materials returned 25, Wages paid 480, Wages accrued 40, Plant installed on site 350 (useful life 5 years, no scrap), Site overheads 160, Head office charges allocated 85, Subcontractor charges 240. Materials on site at year end INR 55 lakh. Work certified INR 18 crore, work uncertified INR 1.2 crore. Cash received from client INR 16.2 crore (90% of certified work). Please provide: (1) Contract account for the year, (2) Value of work-in-progress on balance sheet, (3) Notional profit and estimated profit, (4) Profit to be transferred to P&L using formulae (when less than 25% and 25-50% complete), (5) Treatment under Ind AS 115 percentage-of-completion, (6) Balance sheet extract, (7) Differences between traditional contract costing and Ind AS 115 approach. Format as ICAI-style solved problem in INR lakh.

prompts.title: GST Composition Scheme Analysis
prompts.prompt: Act as a GST advisor with 7 years experience helping small businesses across India choose between regular and composition schemes. I need you to analyze composition scheme applicability and tax impact. Here is the context: Analyze these five small businesses for FY 2025-26: (1) Kirana store in Indore, turnover INR 95 lakh, only intra-state B2C sales, (2) Restaurant in Jaipur, turnover INR 1.2 crore, (3) Manufacturer of steel utensils in Moradabad, turnover INR 85 lakh, (4) Interior designer (service provider) in Bengaluru, turnover INR 48 lakh, (5) Garment trader in Delhi with inter-state supplies of INR 12 lakh out of INR 90 lakh total turnover. For each, assess composition scheme under Section 10 (regular) and Section 10(2A) (services). Please provide: (1) Eligibility analysis with threshold rules, (2) Applicable composition rate (1% traders, 2% manufacturers, 5% restaurants, 6% services), (3) Tax computation vs regular scheme, (4) ITC forfeiture impact, (5) Return filing (CMP-08 quarterly, GSTR-4 annually), (6) Restrictions (no inter-state, no ecommerce, no exempt supplies), (7) Bill of supply vs tax invoice, (8) Recommendation for each. Format as a comparison table in INR.

prompts.title: Hire Purchase and Instalment System
prompts.prompt: Act as a commerce teacher with 11 years experience teaching specialized accounting topics to B.Com students in Indian universities. I need you to prepare accounting entries for a hire purchase transaction in books of both hirer and vendor. Here is the context: On 1 April 2025, Deccan Transport Co, Hyderabad purchased three trucks from Tata Motors on hire purchase. Cash price INR 60,00,000 (INR 20,00,000 per truck). Payment: Down payment INR 12,00,000, balance in 4 annual instalments of INR 15,00,000 each payable at end of each year. Interest rate implicit in the contract to be computed. Depreciation at 20% on WDV method. Deccan's financial year ends 31 March. Please provide: (1) Calculation of interest component in each instalment (use cash price method and annuity method), (2) Interest allocation table showing principal and interest split, (3) Journal entries in books of hirer (Deccan) for all four years, (4) Journal entries in books of vendor (Tata Motors) assuming sales method, (5) Balance sheet extracts at each year end, (6) Treatment of default and repossession scenario, (7) Ind AS 116 implications (whether this would be a finance lease). Format as tables and journals in INR.

prompts.title: ESOP Accounting Under Ind AS 102
prompts.prompt: Act as an Ind AS specialist with 8 years experience advising Indian listed companies on share-based payment accounting. I need you to compute and account for employee stock options under Ind AS 102. Here is the context: Zomato Ltd granted 10,00,000 ESOPs on 1 April 2024 to key employees. Terms: Exercise price INR 80, Fair value of option on grant date INR 25, Vesting period 3 years (graded - 30% at end of Year 1, 30% Year 2, 40% Year 3), Market price on grant INR 110. Performance condition: company must achieve annual revenue growth above 20%. Estimated forfeiture rate 10% at grant date, revised to 8% at end of Year 1 and 6% at end of Year 2. 85,000 options actually forfeited in Year 1, 60,000 in Year 2. 6,50,000 options exercised in Year 3. Please provide: (1) Fair value of total compensation expense computation, (2) Year-wise expense recognition using graded vesting (each tranche treated separately), (3) Journal entries for each year (stock compensation expense, ESOP reserve, cash on exercise), (4) Accounting on exercise and lapse, (5) Disclosure requirements under Ind AS 102, (6) Differences from ICAI Guidance Note intrinsic value method, (7) Income tax implications. Format in INR lakh with detailed workings.

prompts.title: Fraud Risk Assessment SA 240
prompts.prompt: Act as a forensic audit specialist and CA with 15 years experience investigating corporate frauds in India (similar to Satyam, PNB, Yes Bank cases). I need you to design a fraud risk assessment procedure as per SA 240. Here is the context: You are the engagement partner for the statutory audit of Jubilant Retail Ltd, a pan-India retailer with 340 stores, FY 2025-26 turnover INR 2,800 crore. Red flags observed during planning: (1) Sudden CFO resignation two months before year end, (2) Gross margin improved from 32% to 41% without clear reason, (3) Whistleblower complaint about inventory manipulation at three stores, (4) Related party transactions with promoter firms increased 3x, (5) Aggressive revenue recognition on loyalty programme deferred revenue, (6) Cash sales increased 60% while industry trend is toward digital. Please provide: (1) Fraud risk factors categorized per SA 240 (incentive, opportunity, rationalization - the fraud triangle), (2) Assessment of fraud risks at financial statement level and assertion level, (3) Specific audit procedures to address each risk (journal entry testing, retrospective review, unpredictable audit procedures), (4) Discussion with management and TCWG (Section 143(12) reporting), (5) Forensic data analytics tests, (6) Documentation requirements, (7) Reporting to Audit Committee and Board. Format as a structured audit memo referencing SA 240 and Companies Act.
